title: Countertop Potato Bin
summary: A small angled-slat bin with a food-safe finish, sized to sit on a counter and let air circulate around stored potatoes or onions.
description:
The angled dividers do double duty as both the bin's structure and its ventilation — cutting them at 15° instead of square opens a gap between the front slats that lets air move through, which is what keeps stored produce from sweating and rotting early. Cut one divider first and use it as a pattern for the rest, rather than resetting your saw angle four separate times; that's both faster and guarantees all four come out identical.

The feet need slightly more than 3/4" of clearance on the outside for a reason worth keeping: it lets two or more bins stack on top of each other without the upper bin's feet interfering with the lower bin's front slats. Use only food-safe finish, since this sits in direct contact with produce you'll eat.

materials.name: Self-adhesive furniture feet pads
materials.note: Protects the countertop under each foot.

cutList.note: Longest point measurement, one end cut at 15° off square. Cut one first and use it as a pattern for the rest.

steps.title: Cut the angled dividers
steps.body: Set your saw to 15° off square and cut one divider with the long point at 9-3/4". Reset the saw to square (0°) and cut the second divider, using the first one's angled edge as your reference. Repeat for the remaining two dividers. Then set the saw to 30° off square, mark the longer corner as shown in the diagram, and cut it off.

steps.title: Attach the bottom
steps.body: Attach the 1x8 bottom to the dividers with 1-1/4" brad nails and wood glue.

steps.title: Attach the front panel
steps.body: Attach the front 1x4 panel with 1-1/4" brad nails and wood glue.

steps.title: Attach the back panels
steps.body: Attach the two back 1x4 panels the same way, with 1-1/4" brad nails and wood glue.

steps.title: Attach the feet and finish
steps.body: Attach the feet to the bottom, leaving slightly more than 3/4" of clearance on the outside — that extra margin is what lets multiple bins stack. Sand everything smooth, finish with a food-safe stain or leave bare, and add the self-adhesive pads to the bottom of the feet.

images.alt: A small angled-slat countertop bin for storing potatoes or onions
